title: Optimised Inference of Quantum Phenomena in High-Energy Collider Experiments
authors: Hai-Chau Nguyen, Gilberto Tetlalmatzi-Xolocotzi, Carmen Diez Pardos, Otfried Gühne, Matthias Kleinmann
abstract: Entanglement, a fundamental phenomenon of quantum theory, has recently been observed in processes in high-energy physics. This opens new avenues for probing quantum effects in relativistic regimes, but also poses conceptual and technical challenges. We develop a general framework based on shadow tomography techniques for characterising spin-spin correlations in collider experiments. This improves the analysis of spin-spin entanglement, where relativistic motion couples spin and momentum and the momenta of the investigated particles are not under experimental control. As a proof of concept we illustrate the application of our formalism to top quark pair production at the Large Hadron Collider at CERN. The framework, however, is general and flexible and can be readily applied to more complex final states and systems with more particles.
summary: This paper introduces a new method for detecting quantum entanglement in particle collider experiments using shadow tomography. By addressing the challenges of spin-momentum coupling in relativistic regimes, the framework allows for more efficient characterization of spin correlations. The method is demonstrated on top quark production at the LHC.
main_problem: Characterizing spin-spin entanglement in high-energy collider experiments is difficult because relativistic motion couples spin and momentum, and particle momenta are not experimentally controlled.
main_result: The development of a general framework using shadow tomography to efficiently infer spin-spin correlations and entanglement in collider environments.
method: The authors utilize shadow tomography techniques to reconstruct quantum properties from experimental data.
model_or_system: The framework is applied to top quark pair production at the Large Hadron Collider (LHC) as a proof of concept, though it is designed for general high-energy particle systems.
main_assumptions: The framework assumes the applicability of shadow tomography to relativistic regimes where spin and momentum are coupled.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the challenge of entanglement in high-energy physics, presents a new framework based on shadow tomography, and demonstrates its utility using top quark pair production at the LHC.
why_interesting: It demonstrates the application of quantum information protocols (shadow tomography) to extract entanglement signatures from complex, high-energy relativistic systems.
